title: Graph templates: configurable parameterized subgraphs applied to the work DAG
description:
Generalize the hardcoded planning bracket into a **graph-template layer**: named, parameterized subgraphs declared in config and spliced into the work DAG by a generic `jit apply <template> <target>` command. The planning bracket (P→B) becomes the first template (`plan`); the mechanism is generic and domain-agnostic.

## Context

`jit plan` today creates only the planning node `P`; the breakdown node `B` is hand-assembled by the jit-breakdown skill from primitives — an asymmetry, with B-wiring living in skill prose rather than the binary. `jit plan` also leaves `P`'s description empty, which weakens the `plan-review` gate (its prompt reads `issue.description` for criteria) and gives readers no context. The `[planning]` config is effectively a hardcoded two-node bracket declaration.

This epic replaces that with a generic primitive: a **graph template** is a reusable subgraph that the engine instantiates and splices onto bound anchor nodes. The planning bracket is one template; the layer can express other recurring subgraph patterns later.

## Model

- **Graph template** — declared in `.jit/templates.toml`: a named template with
  - **nodes**: each with `role`, `type` (from the type hierarchy), `gates` (gate presets), a `doc` location template, and a `description` template;
  - **internal edges**: `depends_on` expressed by node role (e.g. `breakdown` depends on `planning`);
  - **named anchors**: roles (e.g. `container`) bound to existing issues at apply time; nodes may wire to anchors;
  - **transforms**: a small, fixed vocabulary of edge transforms on anchors (e.g. `move-upstream-to-role`, `redirect`), all DAG-preserving.
- **Static, no deferred edges.** A template fully instantiates at apply: create nodes (fresh ids), wire internal + anchor edges, apply gate presets, run declared transforms. It has no phased/deferred edges; later structural changes (e.g. the breakdown interior splice) happen independently of templates.
- **Templating** — `description` and `doc` fields interpolate from anchor context (container title, id, `[hard]` criteria, labels, doc path), so applying `plan` yields a planning node with a populated description.

## Command

`jit apply <template-name> <anchor bindings>` — generic; `plan` is one template among possible others. Applying `plan` to a container `C` creates `P` (`type:planning`, `plan-review`) and `B` (`type:breakdown`, `coverage-preview` + `breakdown-review`), wires `B→P` and `C→B`, and runs the `move-upstream-to-planning` transform (reproducing today's "move C's upstream deps onto P"). This replaces both the old `jit plan` (P-only) and the skill's hand-wiring of B.

## Success Criteria

- [hard] REQ-01: Graph templates are declared in `.jit/templates.toml` as a named template with nodes (`role`, `type`, `gates`, `doc` template, `description` template), role-based internal `depends_on` edges, and named anchors. No template name or node role is hardcoded in engine logic.
- [hard] REQ-02: `jit apply <template> <anchors>` instantiates a template in one operation — fresh-id nodes, internal + anchor edges, gate presets, declared transforms — and the result is always validated as an acyclic DAG.
- [hard] REQ-03: `description`/`doc` fields interpolate from anchor context; applying `plan` produces a planning node with a non-empty description (closing the empty-P gap).
- [hard] REQ-04: The `plan` template reproduces the planning bracket — applying it to `C` creates `P` (plan-review) and `B` (coverage-preview + breakdown-review) with `B→P` and `C→B` — replacing the split where `jit plan` made P and the skill made B.
- [hard] REQ-05: Templates support multiple named anchors bound at apply; the `plan` template uses one (`container`).
- [hard] REQ-06: The transform vocabulary is small, fixed, and DAG-preserving, with an extensible dispatch. This epic ships only `move-upstream-to-role` (the transform `plan` needs, used as `move-upstream-to-planning`); other transforms (e.g. `redirect`) are deferred.
- [hard] REQ-07: The breakdown engine and the jit-breakdown skill are updated to consume the pre-created `B` and perform only the interior splice (sources→`B`, `C`→sinks); B-creation is removed from skill prose.
- [hard] REQ-08: The template engine is exercised by extensive automated tests against **isolated temporary jit repositories** (never the production `.jit`), and dogfooded on a real container after completion.
- [hard] REQ-09: Existing empty planning-node descriptions (`1eb0bdfd`, `2937919f`) are reseeded from their containers; a manual fix is acceptable — not an engine deliverable.
- [hard] REQ-10: Both real configs (this repo + gf2) are rewritten to the `templates.toml` model; the flat `[planning]` config path is removed (single config path, no desugar shim).

## Non-goals

- General graph rewriting beyond the fixed transform vocabulary (must stay a cycle-checked DAG, small vocabulary).
- Deferred/phased template edges — the breakdown interior splice stays independent of templates.
- Shipping templates other than `plan` this epic (the engine enables them; only `plan` is delivered).
- Changes to the structured-knowledge epic (`25064508`) or to jit-plan-skill behavior beyond the breakdown alignment.

## Relationships

- **Generalizes the planning bracket from epic `2fbd2a82`** (done); no hard dependency edge.
- **jit-plan skill (`eed6750c`) is a beneficiary** — it will call `jit apply plan` and get seeded P+B for free; no edge.
- **Distinct from the structured-knowledge epic (`25064508`)** — that is addressable items/invariants; this is the graph-macro layer.
